title: Chapter 8: Circular Motion - Study Notes
source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics

sections.heading: Uniform Circular Motion
sections.content: If a force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion (velocity), the path will be circular. In circular motion, vector quantities like velocity, linear momentum, acceleration, and force change continuously.
formulas: a_c = v²/r = ω²r = 4π²f²r = (4π²/T²)r (centripetal acceleration)
KE = ½mv² = ½F_c × r (kinetic energy)
Total energy TE = ½F_c × r + ∫-F_c · dr
L = mvr = constant (angular momentum)
τ = dL/dt = 0 (torque = 0)
specialCases: Speed (v) is constant; angular velocity ω is constant.
KE is constant; ΔK = 0; Δω = 0.
Work done and power are zero in uniform circular motion.
Tangential acceleration a_t = 0 (rate of change of speed = 0).
Acceleration is directed radially toward centre (centripetal/normal acceleration).
Work done by centripetal force is always zero in circular motion.

sections.heading: Non-Uniform Circular Motion
sections.content: In non-uniform circular motion, speed, angular velocity, and KE all change. Tangential acceleration a_t ≠ 0 and torque ≠ 0.
formulas: a_t = d|v|/dt (tangential acceleration)
a_total = √(a_c² + a_t²) = √((v²/r)² + (dv/dt)²)
tanβ = a_t/a_c (direction of total acceleration from centripetal)
L = mvr (angular momentum changes)
τ = ma_t × r (torque due to tangential force)
specialCases: If speed increases: a_t is along direction of velocity (angle = 0°).
If speed decreases: a_t is opposite to velocity (angle = 180°).
Work done and power are both due to tangential force only.

sections.heading: Circular Motion on Roads (Banking)
sections.content: For safe turning on roads, vehicles require centripetal force. Banking of roads provides this without relying solely on friction.
formulas: tanθ = v²/(rg) (banking angle for cyclist/vehicle)
tanθ = 2πc/(t²g) where c = circumference, t = time (cyclist on circular path)
h = (v²/rg) × L (super-elevation; h = height of outer edge, L = road width)
v_max = √(μrg) (max speed on level curved road without banking; μ = friction coefficient)
v_max = √(rdg/2h) (max speed for no overturning; d = wheel distance, h = height of CG)
v_min = √(rg tan(θ - α)) (banked road with friction; α = friction angle)
v_max = √(rg tan(θ + α)) (banked road with friction)
specialCases: For banked road: tanθ = v²/rg.
α = angle of friction where tanα = μ.
On convex bridge: v_max = √(rg) (for not losing contact).

sections.heading: Motion in Vertical Circle
sections.content: A body tied to a string of length ℓ rotated in a vertical circle. Both tension and velocity decrease from lowest to highest point.
formulas: T = mg cosθ + mv²/r (tension at angle θ from vertical, measured from lowest point)
T_A = mg + mv_A²/ℓ = T_max (lowest point, θ = 0°)
T_C = mv_C²/r - mg = T_min (highest point, θ = 180°)
T (horizontal) = mv²/r (when string is horizontal, θ = 90°)
v_h = √(v² - 2gh) (velocity at any height h)
h = ℓ(1 - cosθ) (height at angle θ from lowest)
T_max - T_min = 6mg
T_max/T_min = (v_A² + rg)/(v_A² - 5rg) = (v_C² + 5rg)/(v_C² - rg)
|Δv| = √(v_h² + v_A²) = √(2(v² - gℓ)) (change in velocity when string becomes horizontal)
specialCases: Critical velocities: (V_C)_min = √(rg), T_C = 0 (minimum at top)
V_B = V_D = v_max = √(3rg), T = 3mg (at horizontal level)
(V_A)_max = √(5rg), T = 6mg (at bottom, for just completing circle)
h_min = 5r/2 = 5D/4 (minimum height to complete loop from bottom; D = diameter)
Time period T = 2π√(r/g) = 2√r seconds.
T_max - T_min = 6mg always.

sections.heading: Conditions for Different Trajectories (String)
sections.content: Depending on the initial velocity at the lowest point, the body either oscillates, follows a parabolic path, or completes the full circle.
formulas: If v_A < √(2gℓ): velocity becomes zero first → body oscillates. Max height h = v_A²/2g
cosθ = 1 - v_A²/(2gℓ) (angle with vertical when string is slack)
If √(2gℓ) < v_A < √(5gℓ): tension becomes zero first → body leaves circular path, moves as projectile
Height where body leaves: h = (v_A² + gℓ)/(3g)
cosθ = (v_A² - 2gℓ)/(3gℓ) (angle from highest point when tension = 0)
Velocity at departure: v = √((v_A² - 2gℓ)/3)
Max height from lowest: H = (v_A² + gℓ)/(3g) + v²sin²θ/(2g)
If v_A ≥ √(5gℓ): completes full circle
specialCases: Hemispherical bowl: body leaves at h = 2R/3 from centre, h = R/3 from top, cosθ = 2/3 (θ ≈ 48°).
Height from bottom of bowl = 5R/3.
Concave track exerts more normal force than convex track for same vehicle.

sections.heading: Conical Pendulum and Special Cases
sections.content: A conical pendulum has the bob rotating in a horizontal circle while the string makes an angle with the vertical. Also covers coins on rotating tables and spring-mass rotation.
formulas: T = mg/cosθ (tension in conical pendulum string; sinθ = r/ℓ)
tanθ = v²/(rg) (for linear speed of bob)
T_c = 2π√(ℓcosθ/g) = T_s × √cosθ (time period; T_s = simple pendulum period)
F = ½m₁ω²L (force on end of rotating rod of mass m₁, length L, angular velocity ω)
x = mω²ℓ/(k - mω²) (elongation of spring when body rotated; k = spring constant, ℓ = natural length)
x_max = μg/ω² (max distance from centre for coin on rotating table; μ = friction coefficient)
t = √(μg/(α²x)) (time after which coin starts slipping; α = angular acceleration)
n = θ/(2π) = μg/(4παx) (minimum revolutions before slipping)
specialCases: Conical pendulum time period is always less than simple pendulum of same length.
For rotating spring-mass: x = mω²ℓ/(k - mω²); resonance when k = mω².
Satellite centripetal acceleration: a = ω²r ∝ r (for same angular velocity).
